one_line_takeaway: FragBench shows that splitting an attack across separate, context-isolated LLM sessions defeats single-turn safety judges almost completely (pass rate ~60% to ~100% after RL rewriting), but a cross-session interaction-graph classifier (GCN F1=0.956) recovers the compositional signal that per-prompt filtering misses.
key_finding: The single-turn safety judge is near-chance on FragBench by construction (each fragment is individually benign), and the RL rewriter drives it further toward total bypass: aggregate single-turn-judge pass rate rises from ~60% to ~100% within 10 rounds; specifically Opus-as-rewriter with Opus-as-judge gives +26.5% to a 97.4% pass rate, and Opus-as-rewriter with Sonnet-as-judge gives +18.0% to 99.1% (Table 2), while an initial ~25%-per-round refusal rate dropped to 0 refusals from Sonnet/Opus after a few-shot system-prompt rewrite. Despite this near-total single-turn bypass, cross-session graph-based defenses recover the compositional signal well above chance: aggregate event-level F1 ranges from 0.878 (GBT) to 0.956 (GCN), with all four GNN variants (GCN 0.956, GraphSAGE 0.948, GIN 0.937, GAT 0.936) beating the best classical baseline (MLP 0.905) by 3-5 F1 points, while SVM (0.881) and GBT (0.878) trail. The paper's headline claim is therefore that defending against fragmented LLM misuse requires modeling the cross-session interaction graph rather than filtering isolated prompts.
core_contribution: FragBench: a benchmark for 'cross-session fragmentation' attacks, in which an attacker splits a malicious goal into sub-prompts (fragments) sent as separate single-turn requests with no shared context, so each fragment individually passes a single-turn safety judge while the composition realizes a harmful objective. The paper contributes (1) a malicious corpus built from 24 real-world cyber-incident reports (Anthropic, Google GTIG, Microsoft, OpenAI, SentinelLABS/Unit 42, and public incident reports), covering 22 MITRE ATT&CK techniques, split into fragments with 10 disguise 'styles' (6 used in most experiments), (2) a matched synthetic benign cover-session corpus generated with Claude Sonnet 4.5, (3) 'FragBench Attack': an RL-style prompt rewriter that hardens fragments against a single-turn safety judge, paired with an MCP-sandbox execution validator that only accepts assemblies which actually execute the kill chain, and (4) 'FragBench Defense': a typed interaction-graph classifier (union-find chain discovery + GNN/classical-ML scoring) that detects the cross-session attack signal that single-turn judges miss. Code/harness and a two-tier (public + gated) dataset are released on GitHub/HuggingFace.
threat_model: Attacker goal: get an LLM (or an LLM-backed agent with tool/MCP access) to carry out a multi-step malicious campaign (data exfiltration, AI-assisted ransomware, AI-generated malware, supply-chain operations, vulnerability exploitation) without triggering a single-turn safety classifier deployed by the model provider. Attack mechanism: decompose the campaign into 4-16 fragments, each individually benign-looking (optionally disguised via prompting styles such as command_form, sysadmin, educational, compliance_audit, helpdesk), and submit them as independent sessions with no shared context, so the safety judge never sees the full plan. The paper explicitly distinguishes this from prior work: single-session decomposition/jailbreak attacks (DrAttack, SneakyPrompt) and multi-turn jailbreaks with shared chat history (Crescendo, many-shot jailbreaking) both let the model or attacker reassemble context inside one window; FragBench's threat model has zero shared context across fragments, so detection must happen at the user/account level across sessions rather than within a single conversation. Defender goal: without access to ground-truth labels at inference time, link tool-use events across sessions into candidate attack chains and score them as malicious or benign using cross-session graph structure rather than per-prompt judgments.
limitations: The authors' own limitations/ethics section states the synthetic benign dataset (generated by Claude Sonnet 4.5 from a system prompt) 'may be statistically distinguishable from malicious [data] on some axis we did not measure,' and that a comprehensive audit of residual benign-vs-malicious distributional differences is left to future work -- a material caveat since the reported F1 gains rely on separating exactly this benign/malicious pair. Fragment splitting is static (fixed at dataset-construction time) rather than dynamic, which the paper's own footnote 3 notes is easier to verify/test but less realistic than an adaptive real attacker who would vary decomposition granularity on the fly. Per-campaign results are explicitly described as 'sample-size-bounded rather than feature-bounded': the unc2970_operation_dream_job campaign collapses to F1=0.063 across every detector because it has only 16 fragments in the released seed, versus F1>=0.77 for higher-coverage campaigns (ad_discovery, gtg1002, quietvault, ru_malware_clusters). Accuracy is reported as uninformative (uniformly 0.92-0.95) because the benign class dominates the held-out split, so the paper relies on F1 as the real metric. The Claude-model attack experiments (Sonnet 4.6/Opus 4.6 as rewriter and judge) were run on only 20% of campaign data for cost reasons, while DeepSeek was run on 100%, so the headline 97.4%/99.1% Claude figures are not evaluated on the same data volume as the DeepSeek/Llama-Guard-4 comparison rows. The most sensitive, RL-hardened data is withheld behind a gated HuggingFace access tier, limiting independent reproducibility of the strongest attack results by non-vetted researchers.

datasets_used: FragBench malicious corpus: 24 real-world cyber-incident campaigns (from Anthropic, Google GTIG, Microsoft, OpenAI, SentinelLABS/Unit 42, and public incident reports), 22 MITRE ATT&CK techniques, 100 variations per campaign, 25,400 fragments total (~10.58 fragments/variation)
Matched synthetic benign cover-session corpus generated via Claude Sonnet 4.5 (chain lengths 7-14, over-indexed on median length 8), same 6 disguise styles as the malicious side
MCP sandbox execution trace dataset: 649,076 events / 93,280 tool calls generated across 24 specialized MCP servers (sourced from the 'Red-APT' repository)
Public release: HuggingFace LidaSafety/fragbench (seed campaigns, generated variants, benign data) and a gated tier LidaSafety/fragbench-restricted (RL-hardened datapoints, vetted access only)
quantitative_results: FragBench Attack: aggregate single-turn-judge pass rate raised from ~60% to ~100% within 10 RL rounds (per Conclusion)
Opus rewriter + Opus judge: +26.5 points, reaching 97.4% pass rate after RL
Opus rewriter + Sonnet judge: +18.0 points, reaching 99.1% pass rate after RL
Initial refusal rate ~25% of datapoints per round dropped to 0 refusals from Sonnet/Opus after a few-shot system-prompt rewrite of the rewriter
FragBench Defense aggregate event-level F1: GCN 0.956, GraphSAGE 0.948, GIN 0.937, GAT 0.936, MLP 0.905, SVM 0.881, GBT 0.878
Accuracy stayed in a uniform 0.92-0.95 band across all 24 campaigns and all 7 detectors (deemed uninformative due to benign-class dominance)
Per-campaign GCN F1 spanned 0.063 (unc2970_operation_dream_job, only 16 fragments) to 0.814 (ad_discovery and gtg1002)
baselines_compared: Graph neural network detectors: GCN, GraphSAGE, GAT, GIN
Classical ML detectors: SVM, MLP (multilayer perceptron), GBT (gradient-boosted trees)
Rewriter models compared in the attack experiments: Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Opus 4.6, DeepSeek V4 Flash
Judge (safety-classifier) models compared: Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Opus 4.6, Llama Guard 4 (12B, noted as weaker, frequently >90% baseline pass rate before rewriting)
